# Circular Dependencies
*or what to Do When Gradle Can’t Untangle the Graph*

::: {.scenario}
This scenario demonstrates Gradle's handling of circular dependencies.
:::

#### Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibA
```

#### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-10-circular/build.gradle" snippet="s10-deps"}
```

#### Reader Expectation
"It should work."

#### Actual Resolution Results
Resolves successfully.

#### Classpath Reality
Both jars present.

#### Why This Happens
Gradle maintains a set of already-visited nodes during graph traversal. When it encounters a dependency it has already resolved, it stops following that branch, preventing infinite recursion. Unlike Maven, which fails at reactor build time if modules in a multi-project build form a cycle, Gradle handles circular external dependencies gracefully during resolution. Both libraries end up on the classpath.

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
While Gradle tolerates circular dependencies at resolution time, they are still a serious architectural problem. Cycles make it impossible to reason about the build order of modules, force tightly coupled releases, and often indicate that shared abstractions should be extracted into a separate module. In Gradle multi-project builds, circular project dependencies will cause build failures since Gradle needs a linear task execution order.

#### Deterministic Checks

**Fast check (Gradle):**

```bash
./gradlew dependencies
```

**What to look for:**
Gradle will show the circular dependency in the tree, often with an annotation.

**Conclusion:**
While Gradle can resolve circular dependencies, they are a sign of architectural problems and should be fixed.

#### Key Takeaway
Gradle tolerates cycles.

#### Related Scenarios
N/A
